{"covered_dims": {"R1.1": 0.8, "R1.3": 0.9, "R1.4": 0.8, "R1.6": 0.8, "RX.1": 0.9, "RX.2": 0.95, "RX.3": 1.0, "RX.4": 0.5, "RX.5": 0.9}}

Summary: I tightened the prose and reframed the contribution, but I also flagged several places where "make this stronger" cannot honestly be done by rewording alone — the underlying numbers and framing don't yet support the claims being made.

## Revised draft (conservative version)

> We introduce GraphFlow, a message-passing GNN for typed (heterogeneous) graphs in which parameters are shared across edge types rather than instantiated per type. This sharing reduces parameter count by 30–40% relative to a per-type-parameter baseline, with comparable accuracy on three node-classification benchmarks.
>
> On OGB-Products, OGB-Arxiv, and Reddit, GraphFlow reaches 81.7%, 73.4%, and 96.1% accuracy respectively, compared to 81.2%, 73.1%, and 95.9% for our prior GraphFlow-v1 [cite]. We caution that these gaps (0.2–0.5 points) are within the range typically attributable to seed variance on these benchmarks; we therefore frame GraphFlow's contribution as parameter efficiency at matched accuracy, not as a state-of-the-art accuracy improvement.
>
> Section 3 describes the sharing scheme. We hypothesize it acts as a regularizer when some edge types are sparse, an intuition supported by the Section 4 ablation: disabling sharing reduces mean accuracy by ~1.0 point across the three benchmarks. The mechanism is implementable as a small (<100 LoC) modification on top of standard PyTorch Geometric message-passing code.

## Issues you should address before this gets stronger

**1. The headline claim is overstated relative to the evidence.**
The text says the scheme "maintain[s] accuracy," but the numbers reported are *small improvements* (0.2, 0.3, 0.5 points), not parity. Either:
- (a) Lead with parameter efficiency at matched accuracy and treat the small deltas as noise (honest, and what I did above), or
- (b) Defend the gains as real — which requires reporting mean ± std over multiple seeds, and ideally a significance test. Single-number comparisons on OGB are not convincing.

Pick one. The current text tries to have both and undercuts itself.

**2. The novelty framing is thin.**
"Parameter sharing across edge types" is not new in the heterogeneous-GNN literature (e.g., basis/block-decomposition in R-GCN, weight-tying schemes in CompGCN and HGT-style models all live in this space). The draft compares only against the authors' own GraphFlow-v1. Without positioning against this prior art, the contribution looks larger than it is. You need at least a paragraph distinguishing the specific sharing scheme from existing decomposition/tying approaches, and ideally a baseline comparison against one of them.

**3. "30–40% parameter reduction" needs a referent.**
Reduced relative to *what*? GraphFlow-v1? A per-edge-type parameterization? A specific R-GCN configuration? The number is meaningless without naming the baseline and ideally giving absolute parameter counts per benchmark (since the reduction will vary with the number of edge types).

**4. The ablation claim is underspecified.**
"Approximately 1.0% on average" — average over what? Three benchmarks? Multiple seeds? Report per-benchmark numbers with variance, or at minimum say "averaged across the three benchmarks (single run each)."

**5. "Successor to GraphFlow-v1" is awkward and weakens the framing.**
If the only baseline is your own prior model and the gains are within noise, calling this a "successor" invites the reader to ask why it is a separate paper. Either treat v1 as one baseline among several (preferable) or be explicit that this is an incremental architectural study.

**6. The deployment / adoption rhetoric is doing too much work.**
"Useful step forward for practical deployment" and "lowers the bar for adoption" are claims you have not evidenced — there is no deployment study, no wall-clock or memory measurement, no user study. The <100 LoC point is fine as a concrete fact; drop the adoption editorializing or back it with a runtime/mem

projection_input.clarification_question: When you say “Make this stronger,” which primary focus should we take: (A) rhetorical polish and concision without changing structure or claims (R1.6), (B) sharpen the contribution and novelty positioning versus GraphFlow‑v1 and related approaches, possibly with light reordering (R1.1, R1.4, R1.5), or (C) tighten claims to evidence with calibrated language and uncertainty/significance cues (R1.3, RX.1–RX.2)?

rationale.R1.1: A stronger piece often requires re‑articulating the core thesis and gap to foreground the practical and conceptual contribution.
rationale.R1.3: To claim parameter efficiency and maintained accuracy convincingly, the text may need tighter linkage to the reported numbers and ablation.
rationale.R1.4: Perceived strength depends on clearly distinguishing GraphFlow from GraphFlow‑v1 and nearby approaches in terms of delta.
rationale.R1.6: More vigorous, concise phrasing and improved flow can materially enhance the perceived strength of the excerpt.
rationale.RX.1: Any strengthening should still disclose uncertainty (e.g., variance or significance) to avoid false confidence.
rationale.RX.2: Edits must avoid overstating small improvements or generalizing beyond the evidence.
rationale.RX.3: Keep the effort within the bounds of strengthening this excerpt rather than adding new experiments or claims.
rationale.RX.4: Avoid language that could encourage risky or misleading deployment without adequate validation.
rationale.RX.5: Maintain explicit pointers to sections, datasets, and baselines so claims remain traceable after edits.
